quiz_questions.question_text: A 20 N force is applied perpendicular to a wrench 0.3 m from the bolt. What is the torque?
quiz_questions.explanation: τ = rF sinθ = 0.3 × 20 × sin 90° = 6 N·m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A force of 50 N is applied at 30° to a 0.4 m lever arm. What is the torque?
A disk with moment of inertia 2 kg·m² has a net torque of 10 N·m applied. What is its angular acceleration?
quiz_questions.explanation: α = τ/I = 10/2 = 5 rad/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: Two forces create torques of +8 N·m and −3 N·m on a pivot. What is the net torque?
A wheel with I = 0.5 kg·m² accelerates from rest to 10 rad/s in 2 s. What net torque is applied?
A uniform rod of mass 6 kg and length 2 m is pivoted at one end. What gravitational torque acts about the pivot? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Gravity acts at the center (L/2 = 1 m from pivot). τ = mg(L/2) = 6 × 10 × 1 = 60 N·m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A torque of 12 N·m acts on a body with I = 4 kg·m² starting from rest. What is ω after 3 s?
quiz_questions.explanation: α = τ/I = 12/4 = 3 rad/s². ω = αt = 3 × 3 = 9 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A door handle is 0.8 m from the hinges. What minimum perpendicular force produces 16 N·m of torque?
What is the SI unit of torque?
quiz_questions.explanation: Torque is measured in newton-meters (N·m). Although dimensionally equivalent to joules, the unit N·m is used for torque.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disk with I = 0.1 kg·m² experiences angular acceleration of 40 rad/s². What is the net torque?
What is torque?

options.text: The product of mass and angular velocity
The resistance of a body to angular acceleration
The rate of change of angular momentum over distance
The rotational analog of force; the tendency of a force to cause rotation about an axis

quiz_questions.explanation: Torque is the rotational analog of force. It measures how effectively a force causes rotation about an axis, calculated as τ = r × F.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'moment arm' (lever arm) mean?

options.text: The length of the object being rotated
The perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation to the line of action of the force
The distance from the center of mass to the axis of rotation
The radius of the circular path

quiz_questions.explanation: The moment arm (lever arm) is the perpendicular distance from the rotation axis to the line of action of the force. Torque equals force times moment arm.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Newton's Second Law for rotation?

options.text: L = Iω for constant angular momentum
F = ma applied to circular motion
τ_net = Iα; net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law for rotation states τ_net = Iα. It is the rotational analog of F_net = ma.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is static rotational equilibrium?

options.text: A state where the net torque about any axis is zero and the object does not rotate
A state where all forces are balanced but torques are not
A state where angular momentum is conserved
A state where the object spins at constant angular velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Static rotational equilibrium means the net torque about any axis is zero (Στ = 0) and the object has no angular acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a couple in mechanics?

options.text: Two equal and opposite parallel forces separated by a distance, producing pure torque with zero net force
Two forces acting on the same point
Two forces that share the same line of action
A pair of perpendicular forces that balance each other

quiz_questions.explanation: A couple consists of two equal, opposite, parallel forces that produce a net torque but zero net force. The torque of a couple is independent of the pivot point.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the right-hand rule determine for torque?

options.text: The direction of the torque vector
The moment of inertia of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The right-hand rule gives the direction of the torque vector: curl your fingers from r toward F, and your thumb points in the direction of τ = r × F.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the cross product in the context of torque?

options.text: A way to calculate work done by torque
A scalar product giving τ = rF cosθ
A vector operation τ = r × F that produces a vector perpendicular to both r and F
A method of adding two vectors

quiz_questions.explanation: The cross product τ = r × F is a vector operation. Its magnitude is rF sinθ and its direction is perpendicular to both r and F, determined by the right-hand rule.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the standard sign convention for torque?

options.text: Counterclockwise is positive, clockwise is negative
Sign depends only on the direction of force
Clockwise is positive, counterclockwise is negative

quiz_questions.explanation: By standard convention, counterclockwise torques are positive and clockwise torques are negative, consistent with the right-hand rule.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'rotational dynamics' study?

options.text: Only the kinematics of spinning objects
The static balance of forces on a beam
The relationship between net torque and the resulting angular acceleration of a body
The conservation of angular momentum exclusively

quiz_questions.explanation: Rotational dynamics studies how net torques cause angular accelerations, analogous to how net forces cause linear accelerations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the condition for translational equilibrium in a torque problem?

options.text: ΣF = 0; the vector sum of all forces is zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Translational equilibrium requires ΣF = 0. In combined equilibrium problems, both ΣF = 0 (translational) and Στ = 0 (rotational) must hold.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mechanic uses a 0.5 m wrench and applies 80 N perpendicular to it. Her partner uses a 0.25 m wrench. What force must the partner apply at 90° to match the same torque?
quiz_questions.explanation: τ = 0.5 × 80 = 40 N·m. For the partner: F = τ/r = 40/0.25 = 160 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A uniform beam of mass 20 kg and length 4 m rests on two supports at each end. A 60 kg person stands 1 m from the left end. What is the normal force at the left support? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Take torques about the right support. N_L × 4 = 20(10)(2) + 60(10)(3) = 400 + 1800 = 2200. N_L = 2200/4 = 550 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A solid disk (I = ½MR²) of mass 4 kg and radius 0.2 m has a string wrapped around its rim. A constant 5 N tension is applied tangentially. What is the angular acceleration?
A 3 m uniform plank (10 kg) extends 1 m over a table edge. What is the maximum mass that can be placed at the overhanging end without tipping? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Pivot at table edge. The plank's center of mass is 1.5 m from the left end, which is 0.5 m from the table edge on the supported side. Stabilizing torque: 10(10)(0.5) = 50 N·m. Tipping torque: m(10)(1) = 10m. Setting equal: 10m = 50, so m = 5 kg.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pulley of radius 0.1 m and I = 0.02 kg·m² has a rope with tension 30 N on one side and 20 N on the other. What is its angular acceleration?

A sign of mass 8 kg hangs from the end of a uniform horizontal beam (mass 4 kg, length 2 m) attached to a wall. A cable at 30° to the beam supports the far end. What is the cable tension? (g = 10 m/s²)
A merry-go-round (I = 500 kg·m²) is pushed with 200 N tangential force at its 2 m radius rim. Starting from rest, what is its angular speed after 10 s?
An Atwood machine has a solid disk pulley (mass 4 kg, radius 0.1 m) with masses 6 kg and 2 kg. What is the system's acceleration? (g = 10 m/s²)
A turntable (I = 0.4 kg·m²) spins at 6 rad/s. A frictional torque of 0.2 N·m acts on it. How long until it stops?
quiz_questions.explanation: α = −τ/I = −0.2/0.4 = −0.5 rad/s². Time to stop: t = ω₀/|α| = 6/0.5 = 12 s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 m ladder (15 kg) leans against a frictionless wall at 60° to the ground. A 75 kg person stands at the ladder's midpoint. What is the friction force at the ground? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Torques about the base: F_wall × 5 sin 60° = 15(10)(2.5 cos 60°) + 75(10)(2.5 cos 60°). F_wall × (5√3/2) = 187.5 + 937.5 = 1125. F_wall = 1125/(2.5√3) = 450/√3 = 150√3 ≈ 260 N. Friction at the ground equals F_wall = 150√3 ≈ 260 N.
